conversation.message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.

It sounds like you're feeling pretty hopeless about quitting this time around, and it's understandable given your past experiences. What is it about quitting that feels so hard to you, even after trying before?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.
You've been smoking for 20 years, and weight concerns are a big motivator for you to quit. What are some situations or emotions that make you reach for a cigarette?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.
You're feeling stuck and not ready to quit smoking right now, which is totally understandable. Can you tell me what you usually do when you're feeling stressed or anxious, like you are now?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?
You're not making any promises, and that's okay. I understand you're still hesitant. What are some situations that usually make you feel like reaching for a cigarette?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.

I can sense a bit of frustration and reluctance in your tone, which is completely understandable given your past experiences with quitting. What triggers or situations make you reach for a cigarette, even if you're just feeling overwhelmed or frustrated?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.

You seem unsure about why you're here, but you've been smoking for 20 years and say it's a big part of who you are. Can you tell me more about what that means to you, and what you think will happen if you stop smoking?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.
When you tried quitting in the past, were there any specific situations or emotions that made you feel more like reaching for a cigarette?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.
It sounds like your doctor and family aren't very supportive of your quit attempt, which can be really tough. How do you think that lack of support is affecting your motivation to quit?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.

Your feelings of failure are understandable, but they don't define your worth or ability to quit. Can you think back to one of your previous attempts to quit, what was it that gave you a glimmer of hope, a small sense of accomplishment, or a reason to keep going?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.
I appreciate your willingness to take a small step towards quitting. What's the one thing you're most scared will happen if you try to reduce your smoking?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.
You're feeling hopeless about quitting again, and that's understandable given your past experiences. Can you tell me more about what happened the last time you tried to quit?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.

You've had a tough time quitting in the past, and weight concerns are a big worry for you. Can you tell me more about what happens when you do start smoking again after trying to quit - is it always the same situation or is it triggered by something specific?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.
You're feeling uncertain about quitting right now, which can be really tough. What do you think triggers your desire to smoke when you're feeling stressed or overwhelmed?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?
You're hesitant, but willing to take small steps. Can you think of any situations or times when you usually find yourself reaching for a cigarette?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.

You seem a bit resistant to the idea of exploring your triggers and making small steps towards quitting. What's behind this reluctance, and is there something specific that's making you feel that way?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.

You're not feeling very motivated to quit right now, and it's clear that past attempts haven't been successful. Can you tell me more about what's been going on in your life lately that might be making it hard for you to want to quit smoking?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

You've tried several methods to quit before, and they didn't work out as planned. What specifically happens when you get anxious or depressed - is it right after you stop smoking or more gradually over time?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

I understand that your doctor and family may not fully understand the challenges you're facing with quitting smoking. You mentioned that you've tried various methods in the past, but they didn't work out. Have you considered avoiding social situations where people are smoking, at least for now, to make quitting a bit easier?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.

I understand that you're feeling uncertain and discouraged by your past attempts. Can you think of anything from those previous attempts that might be worth trying again, even if it was just a small thing that helped you feel a little better?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.

Now that we're working together on a plan, I want to explore a specific strategy that might help you manage your social triggers. Would you be open to keeping a journal to track when and where you tend to feel the urge to smoke in social situations?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit hopeless about quitting again. Can you tell me what made you decide to come to EDosthi this time, despite feeling like quitting hasn't worked for you in the past?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.

You've been smoking for 20 years, and one of the things that's been holding you back from quitting is your fear of gaining weight. When you do crave a cigarette, is it usually at certain times of the day or in specific situations, like after meals or when you're feeling stressed?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.

You're feeling uncertain about quitting right now and worried that it won't work out. What are some situations or feelings that make you want to reach for a cigarette, even though you're not sure you're ready to quit?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?
You're still hesitant, and that's okay. Before we think about what small steps we can take, can you tell me a little bit more about what's holding you back from quitting right now?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit resistant to this process. What's making you feel like you need to "get this over with"?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.
You've been smoking for 20 years and feel like it's a big part of who you are. Can you tell me more about what you think would make it hard for you to give up smoking?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

I sense that you've struggled with both physical and emotional challenges when trying to quit in the past. Have you considered exercising regularly as a way to improve your mood and reduce cravings for cigarettes?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

Given your frustration with your doctor, family, and friends not understanding the challenges of quitting, how would you feel if we came up with a plan that involved politely telling them you can't accept cigarettes from them, and instead, ask for support in quitting?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.
I hear that you're feeling discouraged and unsure about quitting again. What do you think it would take for you to feel a little more hopeful about your chances of success this time around?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.

Given your history with quitting and your concerns about feeling like you're going it alone, I want to suggest keeping a diary of your triggers and cravings to help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies. Would you be willing to try that?

Honestly, I'm feeling really frustrated. I've been trying to quit for weeks now, but it's just not sticking. I've been smoking for about 10 years, and I know it's not good for me, but it's hard to stop.

I can sense your frustration, and it's understandable that quitting after 10 years of smoking is tough. What specifically is happening around the same time you're trying to quit that's making it harder for you to stick to your goal?

Well, I've been feeling really guilty about smoking around my niece and nephew. They're always asking me why I smell like smoke, and it breaks my heart. I want to be a good role model for them, and I don't want to die young from some smoking-related illness.
That must be really tough for you, feeling guilty in front of your niece and nephew. What's a typical day like for you - do you usually smoke at certain times or in specific situations?
Hmm...I'd say about a 6? I mean, I've tried before and failed, so I'm not sure if I can really do it this time.
Let's explore what might be holding you back, Rachel. What do you think you're missing that would make it a 7 out of 10 in terms of your confidence to quit?

I think I need more support. My friends and family are all smokers, so it's hard to stay motivated when everyone around me is still smoking. And as a freelancer, I work from home, so I don't have coworkers to support me either.

Rachel, given your need for support, I think it would be helpful to have someone to hold you accountable and provide encouragement. Would you be open to having a friend or family member join our sessions, or at least check in with you regularly to support your quitting efforts?
Yeah, I've thought about it, but I don't know...I'm not really sure where to start or if it would even help.

I understand you're not sure about joining a support group or talking to a counselor, Rachel. What if I suggested we start by keeping a small notebook to track when your cravings happen and what's going on around you at those times - might that help you identify patterns and feel more in control?
Yeah, okay. I guess it couldn't hurt to try.
Now that you're open to exploring support options, let's start with the basics: would you prefer to start with a weekly support group or one-on-one counseling sessions?

Honestly, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's hard. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit a couple of times before. It didn't work out. I guess I'm just worried about the cost of quitting. I've heard those prescription medications can be really expensive.
You've been smoking for 15 years and have tried to quit a couple of times before, but it didn't work out. Can you think of any specific situations or emotions that make you more likely to smoke?

Well, like I said, the cost is a big deal. I'm not sure if my insurance will cover the medications or the nicotine replacement therapy. And even if it does, I'm not sure if I can afford the copays. And then there's the fear of failure. I've tried to quit before, and I didn't make it. I don't want to disappoint my family and friends again. As for my smoking habits, I usually smoke about a pack a day.

You mentioned your family and friends are counting on you to quit smoking, and that's a big motivator for you. Have you noticed any patterns or situations where you tend to smoke more, such as when you're stressed or around specific people?
Okay, that makes sense. I didn't realize my nicotine dependence level was that high. And yeah, let's talk about those options. I want to know what might work best for me.

I'm glad we're exploring the options for quitting together. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you think about trying nicotine replacement therapy or other medications to help with quitting?
Okay, that sounds good. I think I'd like to try the nicotine replacement therapy. And what about counseling and support groups? How do those work?

You're considering trying nicotine replacement therapy, which is a great first step. When do you usually smoke your first cigarette in the day, and do you have a specific time of day when you feel the urge to smoke most strongly?
Yeah, I knew it was bad for me, but I didn't realize it was that bad.
You mentioned it's bad for you, but you still smoke a pack a day. Can you tell me more about what's happening in your daily routine that makes it hard for you to quit?
Yeah, that sounds like a good plan. I can do that. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I can actually do this this time.
You mentioned feeling more confident about quitting this time, but you've tried quitting before and it didn't work out. Can you tell me more about what made those previous attempts unsuccessful?
Honestly, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think I need to quit smoking. It's not a big deal. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I'm fine.
I sense some resistance, it sounds like you're not sure why you came here or if quitting is necessary. Can you tell me what makes you feel like you're "fine" despite 15 years of smoking?
Quitting? Nah, I don't think so. I've tried it before, and it didn't work out. I don't want to gain weight, and I don't want to give up the social aspect of smoking. It's part of who I am, you know?
I hear that quitting doesn't appeal to you because it means giving up the social aspect of smoking. What kind of social situations typically involve smoking for you?
I don't know...I've seen friends who quit and they just ballooned up. I don't want that to happen to me. And what about when I'm out with friends at the bar? Smoking is just part of the experience.
Let's take a step back and focus on your concerns about social situations and weight gain. When you're out with friends at the bar, what's the first thing you think about when you see others smoking?
It's just...I'm a bartender, and smoking is part of the culture. My friends all smoke, and it's just what we do. If I quit, I'd feel like I'm missing out or something.

It sounds like being a bartender and being around your friends who smoke makes it really hard for you to imagine quitting. Can you think of one specific thing you could do differently at the bar that would help you feel more in control when others are smoking?
I guess so...but I'm still not sure I want to quit.
Let's explore your past experiences with quitting. What did you find most challenging about those attempts, and what did you learn from them that you could apply this time around?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit before, but I started again. I don't know why I can't just stay quit. It's like I'm stuck in this cycle.
I can sense the discouragement you're feeling, and that's understandable. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?

Well, I was doing okay for a while. I had cut down to just a few cigarettes a day, and I was feeling pretty proud of myself. But then I started getting really stressed with work, and I just fell back into old habits. I'd take a break, and the next thing I knew, I was smoking again. It's like my brain just automatically goes to cigarettes when I'm feeling overwhelmed.

I can sense your frustration, and it's understandable that you'd feel that way after trying to quit before. What's been going through your mind lately when you think about quitting for good, is it something specific that's holding you back?
Hmm, I don't think I've tried those before. How do they work?
What are your thoughts about trying something new to help you quit, and what's holding you back from considering it?
Okay, that sounds like it could be helpful.
You're considering trying some new approaches to help with quitting. What's driving you to want to quit smoking now, more than when you tried before?
Yeah, that would be great.
You're open to exploring ways to manage weight gain, that's a great step. What specifically is it about gaining weight that concerns you the most?
I suppose I could try to cut down to just one cigarette break a day instead of two or three. That might be a start.

You're considering taking a small step by cutting down to just one cigarette break a day. What's driving you to want to reduce your smoking, is it something specific that's happened or just a general feeling that you need to make a change?
Yeah, that sounds good. I think I need all the help I can get right now.
You're acknowledging you need help, which is a huge step. What do you think is the hardest part about quitting for you right now?

Honestly, I'm not doing great. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I've tried to quit a few times, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself. I feel like I should be able to quit, but it's just so hard.

It sounds like you're really struggling with feelings of frustration and self-doubt when it comes to quitting smoking. What are some specific situations or emotions that make you reach for a cigarette?

It's just...I have depression and anxiety, and smoking seems to help me cope with those feelings. But at the same time, I know it's making things worse. And I've tried to quit before, but I always gain weight, and that just makes me feel even more miserable. And my friends and family all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation. And to top it all off, my partner doesn't support me in my quit attempts, so I feel like I'm doing it all on my own.

I see that you've tried quitting before but still struggle with it, and it seems like you're looking for a different approach this time around. Would you be open to writing down some reasons why quitting smoking is important to you, to help keep you motivated when things get tough?

Well, when I'm feeling anxious or overwhelmed, smoking just seems to calm me down, you know? It's like a temporary escape from my emotions. But I know it's not a healthy way to deal with things, and it's just making everything worse in the long run.

You're looking for a temporary escape from your emotions. That makes sense. Have you ever tried taking a short walk outside when you feel overwhelmed, to see if that helps calm you down without reaching for a cigarette?
Yeah, I guess I never thought about that. I've always just focused on quitting smoking, but maybe I need to focus on dealing with the underlying issues first. That makes sense.

I'm glad we're on the same page, Rachel. Given that you've decided to focus on addressing the underlying issues, I'd like to suggest a simple yet powerful technique to help you cope with those feelings of anxiety and overwhelm. Would you be open to practicing saying "no" to smoking when you feel the urge to smoke, and reminding yourself why you decided to quit?

Honestly, I'm not really sure. I mean, I know smoking's bad for me and all, but I've been doing it for years and I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. Plus, I've got a lot of other stuff going on in my life right now.
That can be overwhelming, Alex. When you do reach for a cigarette, what's usually going on in the moment - are you with others, feeling stressed, or in a specific situation?
I guess I just feel like it's not good for me, and I should probably stop. But like I said, I've got a lot of other stuff going on, and I'm not sure I'm ready to take on quitting smoking too.

You're feeling a bit overwhelmed with everything going on in your life, and that's making it hard for you to consider quitting smoking. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "a lot of other stuff going on" - is it work, relationships, or something else that's causing you stress?
Yeah, I've heard that before, but I'm not sure I believe it. I mean, I've smoked for years and I'm fine.

I understand that you're not sure you believe the benefits of quitting, and you feel like you're fine despite smoking for years. Can you tell me more about your daily routine, are there any specific times or situations where you tend to smoke?
Yeah, I guess so. I'm worried that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse.

It sounds like you're worried that quitting smoking might impact your depression and anxiety, which can be really scary. What specifically are you imagining will happen if you quit smoking and it affects your mental health?
Okay, that sounds like it might be helpful.
You seem more open to the idea of quitting smoking now. What specific situations or emotions make you reach for a cigarette?
Yeah, I knew that. But like I said, I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit.

You've mentioned weight concerns before, Alex - what specific situations or times of the day do you think might trigger you to crave cigarettes, making you reach for them to try to manage your hunger or weight?
Okay, I think I'm willing to listen and learn more.
That's great that you're willing to listen and learn more. What situations or emotions usually make you reach for a cigarette, and how do you feel in those moments?

Honestly, I'm really struggling. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The longest I lasted was three months, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself.

You've been trying to quit for a while now, and it's great that you're acknowledging your frustration with yourself. What do you think triggers you to go back to smoking after you've made progress towards quitting?

My daughter. She's getting older, and I don't want her to see me smoking. I'm worried about the example I'm setting for her. And to be honest, I'm also worried about my health. I've been experiencing some breathing issues lately, and I know it's related to smoking.
You're motivated by your concern for your daughter's well-being and your own health. What specific situations or emotions make you reach for a cigarette when you're trying to quit?

Well, that's the thing. I don't really have one. My husband is a social smoker, and my friends all smoke. They don't understand why I can't just cut down instead of quitting altogether. And my work schedule is so busy, I don't have time for support groups or counseling.
Who in your life could be that person for you, someone who knows you're trying to quit and will support you when you're struggling, but not necessarily by being around you all the time?
Yeah, I've been using some online forums and apps, but it's hard to connect with others. It feels like I'm going through this alone. And sometimes the advice they give just doesn't resonate with me.
I sense your frustration with the online resources not being helpful. What is it about the advice that doesn't resonate with you - is it the approach, the suggestions, or something else?
Cravings. I get these intense cravings, especially during stressful work meetings or when I'm driving. And sometimes I just give in to them.

It sounds like those cravings are really tough for you, especially in certain situations. Would you be open to trying a simple change of environment when you feel a craving coming on, like stepping outside for some fresh air?
Yeah, I'm willing to try anything at this point. I just want to quit for good this time.
You're taking a brave step forward today, and I have hope that you'll find the strength and resilience you need to overcome this challenge.

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two years, but then I started again after a night out with friends. Now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day. It's frustrating because I know I can quit, but it feels like I'm stuck in this cycle.

I can sense how frustrating it must be to feel like you're stuck in this cycle again. What are some moments or situations that tend to make you reach for a cigarette, like when you're with friends or under stress?

Well, I was out with friends at a bar, and we were all having a few drinks. It's just so easy to smoke when I'm drinking. I feel like I can relax and be myself around my friends when we're all smoking together. It's like it's a part of who I am when I'm socializing.

It sounds like the social aspect of drinking with friends is a big part of why you reach for a cigarette. Would you be open to having a supportive person in your life, someone who knows you're trying to quit, to call or talk to when you're in those situations?

Honestly, I don't know. I feel like I'd be the only one not smoking, and it would be awkward. Plus, I'm worried that if I quit, I'll just get anxious and irritable, and that would be even worse than smoking.

I think it's really interesting that you're worried about being the only one not smoking. Have you considered practicing a simple phrase like "I'm not smoking anymore" or "I'm trying to quit" when someone offers you a cigarette in social situations, to help you feel more confident and prepared?

I guess it would be better for my health, and I wouldn't have to worry about the smell or the cost. But it's hard to see past the short-term struggles. I just feel like I've failed already, so why bother trying again?

Since we're in the Planning phase, I'm supposed to gently introduce a relevant coping strategy or technique. Considering the patient's struggles with social situations and past attempts, I'd like to suggest practicing politely saying no to cigarettes when offered, and reminding themselves why they decided to quit. 

How do you think you might handle a situation like that next time, where you're out with friends and someone offers you a cigarette?

Look, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think quitting is a big deal. I've been smoking for 25 years, and it's not like it's killing me or anything. I enjoy it, it helps me relax, and it's just part of who I am.

It sounds like you're not feeling a strong motivation to quit smoking right now, and that it's really become a part of your daily routine and identity. What are some specific situations or times when you find yourself reaching for a cigarette?

Well, like when I'm dealing with a difficult project or client, it calms me down. And when I'm on a break at work or waiting for a job to start, I just smoke out of habit, I guess. And, you know, when I'm with friends or at parties, it makes me feel more relaxed and social. But, honestly, I don't think I can quit. I've tried before, and it didn't work. I don't think I can do it.
Alex, you mentioned that quitting doesn't seem like a priority for you, but what is it about the idea of quitting that feels so daunting or unachievable to you?

Oh, I don't know... I guess I'd have more money, and my wife would be off my back about it. But, to be honest, I don't think it would make a huge difference. I've got other issues, like my depression and anxiety, that are way more important to deal with. And, like I said, smoking helps me cope with those things. Plus, I don't think I can quit without quitting drinking and pot too, and I'm not willing to do that.

I understand you feel smoking helps you cope with your depression and anxiety, Alex. Would you be open to exploring other relaxation techniques that don't involve nicotine, such as deep breathing or exercise, that you could use in those situations?
I don't know... I'm not sure I believe that. But, I guess it couldn't hurt to talk about it. But, don't expect me to quit anytime soon.

Considering your past attempts to quit haven't worked out, I'm wondering if there was anything from those attempts that you found helpful, no matter how small, that we could build upon this time around.

Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been trying to quit for a while now, but it's just so hard. I've tried cold turkey, nicotine gum, and even prescription medication, but nothing seems to stick. I'm worried that I'll never be able to quit.
It sounds like you've been trying to quit for a while, and it's really tough for you. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?

Well, I know it's bad for my health, and I'm worried about the impact it's having on my lungs and heart. But to be honest, it's also the stress and anxiety that comes with it. I use tobacco as a way to cope with my emotions, and I'm not sure what I'll do without it. I'm afraid that quitting will just make things worse.
I can see how using tobacco helps you cope with stress and anxiety, Rachel. Have you ever tried doing something physical to calm down, like taking a short walk, before reaching for a cigarette?

Honestly, I'm struggling. I quit about a month ago, but it's been tough. I've been using nicotine gum, but I still find myself thinking about smoking all the time. And to be honest, I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always had a hard time with my weight, and I'm scared that quitting smoking will make it worse.
I can sense your frustration and concern about weight gain. How many cigarettes were you smoking per day before you tried to quit?

I guess I just got tired of feeling like a slave to cigarettes. I was smoking 15-20 a day, and it was taking a toll on my health and my wallet. And to be honest, I was getting tired of the smell and the way it made me feel. But it's hard because I really enjoy smoking. I miss the social aspect of it, and I miss the ritual of it. It's hard to imagine relaxing without a cigarette.
You mentioned you miss the social aspect of smoking and the ritual of it. When you're with friends or in social situations, what typically happens that makes you crave a cigarette?

I think it would be amazing. I would feel so much healthier, and I would be proud of myself for overcoming this addiction. But I'm scared that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse. I've struggled with those conditions for a while, and I'm worried that quitting smoking will make them harder to manage.
You're concerned about how quitting smoking might affect your depression and anxiety. What specific situations or feelings trigger your anxiety and depression right now?
Yeah, I think that would be helpful. I just feel like I'm stuck and I don't know how to move forward.
You feel stuck, and it's hard to imagine moving forward without cigarettes. What specifically makes you feel like you're stuck right now?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two weeks six months ago, but I ended up starting again. It's just so hard. I feel like I've failed.
I can sense your frustration and disappointment, but let's start with the basics. How much tobacco do you typically use in a day?

Yeah... I went out with friends to a bar, and we were all having drinks and laughing... and someone offered me a cigarette. I didn't even think twice about it. It was like my brain just shut off, and I was back to my old habits. And then, of course, one cigarette turned into a pack, and now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day.

It sounds like a tough situation, and it's great that you're recognizing the pattern of how it led back to smoking again. What is it about quitting that's really important to you right now, and what would you gain by successfully quitting?
